claim: Simon Willison maintains a prolific near-daily posting cadence on simonwillison.net through April 2026, with 20+ posts in April alone covering new model releases, hands-on tool tests, and his recurring 'pelican on a bicycle' SVG benchmark.
quote: DeepSeek V4 - almost on the frontier, a fraction of the price (April 24, 2026); A pelican for GPT-5.5 via the semi-official Codex backdoor API (April 23, 2026); Where's the raccoon with the ham radio? (ChatGPT Images 2.0) (April 21, 2026)

claim: Sebastian Raschka publishes long-form LLM architecture deep-dives on Ahead of AI roughly monthly, with 2026 posts including 'A Visual Guide to Attention Variants in Modern LLMs' (Mar 22) and 'My Workflow for Understanding LLM Architectures' (Apr 18).
quote: Apr 18 - My Workflow for Understanding LLM Architectures; Apr 4 - Components of A Coding Agent; Mar 22 - A Visual Guide to Attention Variants in Modern LLMs; Feb 25 - A Dream of Spring for Open-Weight LLMs

claim: Lilian Weng's Lil'Log has gone largely dormant in 2025-2026: the most recent post is 'Why We Think' from May 1, 2025, with prior posts spanning 2024.
quote: Why We Think - May 1, 2025; Reward Hacking in Reinforcement Learning - November 28, 2024; Extrinsic Hallucinations in LLMs - July 7, 2024

claim: Chip Huyen's huyenchip.com blog has slowed to a near-halt in 2025-2026, with the last visible posts being 'Common pitfalls when building generative AI applications' (Jan 16, 2025) and 'Agents' (Jan 7, 2025).
quote: Jan 16, 2025: Common pitfalls when building generative AI applications; Jan 7, 2025: Agents. Chip maintains an irregular publishing schedule.

claim: Eugene Yan publishes long, practical essays on eugeneyan.com roughly every 4-8 weeks, with reading times spanning 2-66 minutes; recent pieces cover product evals, principal-IC career advice, and recsys.
quote: Product Evals in Three Simple Steps (23 Nov 2025) - 9 minutes; Advice for New Principal Tech ICs (19 Oct 2025) - 14 minutes. Posts range from 2-66 minutes of reading time, with most substantive pieces requiring 8-23 minutes.

claim: Vicki Boykis posts roughly twice monthly on vickiboykis.com in 2026, mixing applied ML infrastructure pieces like 'Querying 3 billion vectors' (Feb 21) with reflective programming-culture essays.
quote: Apr 20, 2026 - Build yourself flowers; Apr 13, 2026 - Mechanical sympathy; Apr 6, 2026 - On Programming Joy and Octocat; Feb 21, 2026 - Querying 3 billion vectors

claim: Andrej Karpathy still publishes occasional deep posts on karpathy.github.io: 'microgpt' (Feb 12, 2026) introduces a 200-line dependency-free Python GPT trainer.
quote: microgpt - takes 200 lines of pure, dependency-free Python to train and inference GPT. This is a single file with no dependencies that trains and inferences a GPT

claim: Hamel Husain's hamel.dev focuses on practical AI engineering and evals; 2026 posts include 'Evals Skills for Coding Agents' (Mar 2) and 'The Revenge of the Data Scientist' (Mar 26).
quote: 3/26/26 - The Revenge of the Data Scientist; 3/2/26 - Evals Skills for Coding Agents. Husain's writing tends toward practical, opinion-driven analysis of AI engineering topics.

claim: Jay Alammar has frozen the GitHub-hosted jalammar.github.io and moved new visual-explainer posts (e.g. 'The Illustrated DeepSeek R-1') to Substack while at Cohere.
quote: Alammar is freezing his GitHub blog (jalammar.github.io) and moving to Substack, where he finds the authoring experience more convenient, and has recently posted The Illustrated DeepSeek R-1.

claim: Christopher Olah's colah.github.io has not had new posts since 2021; his current writing output is primarily through Anthropic's interpretability/transformer-circuits research rather than the personal blog.
quote: There is no evidence in this webpage that Christopher Olah has posted in 2025 or 2026. The visible posts span from 2014-2021

claim: Finbarr Timbers writes Artificial Fintelligence, a technically detailed AI-research Substack read by researchers at OpenAI, DeepMind, Midjourney and academic labs, with posts continuing through mid-2025.
quote: detailed articles about the frontiers of AI research. The publication is read by over 5000 researchers at OpenAI, DeepMind, Midjourney, Google, Stanford, Berkeley

claim: Cameron R. Wolfe (Netflix Research) writes Deep (Learning) Focus, releasing accessible deep-learning research overviews several times a week to tens of thousands of subscribers.
quote: Wolfe teaches readers about deep learning research via short, understandable overviews that are released a few times each week

claim: An April-2026 ranking of tech/AI blogs places Sebastian Raschka first (score 86/100), with Josh Comeau and Simon Willison tied at 82, and notes 23 of the top 25 entries are individual practitioners rather than corporate blogs.
quote: Sebastian Raschka holds the top spot with an Awesome Score of 86 out of 100, followed by Machine Learning Mastery at 84 and Josh Comeau and Simon Willison tied at 82... only two entries in the top 25 are multi-author publications

claim: Sander Dieleman (DeepMind) publishes infrequent but paper-length essays on diffusion and generative modeling at sander.ai, with the most recent visible post dated April 15, 2025.
quote: On April 15, 2025, Sander Dieleman published a blog post about generative models... His blog at sander.ai focuses extensively on diffusion models and generative modeling
